text: Neuromuscular fatigue in ultra-marathon running has both central (CNS) and peripheral (muscular) components, and fatigue resistance is the critical performance determinant.

Central regulation of pacing in ultra-endurance running depends not only on afferent signals from muscles and peripheral organs, but also on peripheral fatigue and spinal/supraspinal inhibition, since these alterations require higher central drive for a given power output.

Fatigue measured via maximal voluntary contractions and electrical/magnetic stimulation does not necessarily predict submaximal performance limitation or pacing regulation during ultra-endurance running.

Rate of perceived exertion acts as the primary regulator of pacing strategy in ultra-marathon running, modulated by physiological filling rate, recovery capacity, and a security reserve that prevents physiological damage.

Environmental conditions, sleep deprivation, mental fatigue, pain-killers, psychostimulants, cognitive strategies, and nutritional strategies all modulate ultra-running performance through their effects on the central fatigue regulation system.

Ultra-marathon fatigue characteristics are highly specific to exercise intensity, duration, muscle groups involved, and contraction type, making direct transfer from laboratory fatigue protocols to race performance unreliable.
